source: Built from official bulk sources: goarmy cmtjobs servlet, airforce.com careers API, NAVMC 1200.1L, and DoD COOL. No figure inferred; missing fields are absent, not invented. Pay is deliberately excluded — it is a function of paygrade, not of the job.

specialties.name: Boatswain's Mate
specialties.what_it_is: navyenlisted.com reports a 60-month (5-year) initial service obligation for BM, longer than several other ratings' typical 4-year first contract — not independently confirmed against a primary Navy source this session.

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: Navy has no single named line score for this cluster — ratings sum raw ASVAB subtest standard scores (per data/interest-taxonomy.json: 'Navy: no line score — per-rating subtest sums'). Secondary sources (cogn-iq.org, gyfted.me) report BM requires VE+AR+MK+AS ≥ 175 AND MK+AS+AO ≥ 135 (both must be met simultaneously). UNVERIFIED at primary-source level — MyNavyHR (mynavyhr.navy.mil) returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch this session; confirm the current cutoff with a recruiter or the official BM rating page before relying on it.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: Not typically required for the general rating. Assignment to a Visit, Board, Search, and Seizure (VBSS) team or certain security-adjacent boat-crew billets may require one — not confirmed to primary-source level this session.

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: Conflicting figures found this session: one source states VE+AR ≥ 76 on the official CS community overview; another states VE+AR ≥ 88. UNVERIFIED — the two figures were not reconciled against a directly fetched primary Navy source (mynavyhr.navy.mil returned HTTP 403 on fetch this session). Consistent with data/interest-taxonomy.json, which notes the Navy uses per-rating subtest sums rather than a single published line score for this cluster. Confirm the current cutoff with a recruiter or the Navy COOL/CS rating page directly.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: Not required for the rating generally. Assignment to Flag Mess (serving admirals/senior executives) or the White House Mess (serving the President) is a competitive special-duty assignment that requires a background investigation/security clearance — a genuine prestige/advancement path within this rating. (navycs.com, fetched 2026-07-12)

specialties.name: Cyber Warfare Technician
specialties.what_it_is: CORRECTION to this project's interest-taxonomy.json: the taxonomy's listed example, 'CTN - Cryptologic Technician Networks,' was disestablished June 28, 2023; its personnel and duties transferred to the newly established Cyber Warfare Technician (CWT) rating used in this record. U.S. citizenship required.

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: No single Navy 'line score' - Navy ratings qualify via ASVAB subtest-sum combinations, consistent with interest-taxonomy.json's note that Navy has no line-score system. For CWT: AR + 2(MK) + GS >= 255, OR VE + AR + MK + MC >= 235, OR CT + MK + VE >= 173 AND CT >= 60 (CT = Cyber Test, a supplemental Navy aptitude test beyond the standard ASVAB). Search-sourced (ratemyasvab.com); not independently confirmed against a primary Navy COOL/NAVET document this session.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: Top Secret/Sensitive Compartmented Information (TS/SCI) required. Confirmed via official navy.com fetch. This is a hard gate independent of ASVAB score: per SEAD 4 (the government-wide National Security Adjudicative Guidelines), common disqualifiers include foreign influence/preference (close foreign-national contacts, dual citizenship, foreign-government ties), financial considerations (unresolved delinquent debt, bankruptcy pattern, tax delinquency), and drug involvement/substance misuse (recent illegal drug use or a pattern of misuse) - a qualifying ASVAB/CT score does not offset a disqualifying finding under these guidelines.

specialties.name: Intelligence Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: Under DoD's 13 Adjudicative Guidelines (Security Executive Agent Directive 4 / 32 CFR Part 147), none of the following are automatic mechanical disqualifiers under the 'whole person' standard, but they are the dominant real-world reasons applicants and incumbents are denied or lose eligibility, and each can rule out this specialty even with a fully qualifying ASVAB score: (1) unresolved foreign contacts, foreign relatives in certain countries, foreign property/financial interests, or foreign romantic ties that create potential foreign leverage (Guideline B, Foreign Influence); (2) a pattern of serious financial delinquency -- unresolved debt (especially over $10,000), recent bankruptcy, wage garnishment, or repeated late payment patterns (Guideline F; financial issues are cited as roughly 45% of DoD clearance denials); (3) illegal drug use, misuse of prescription medication, or continued use after applying (Guideline H); and (4), treated especially seriously, deliberately concealing or falsifying any of the above -- including an UNREPORTED dual citizenship or undisclosed foreign contact -- during the application or investigation (Guideline E, Personal Conduct). A 16-24-year-old considering this cluster should treat clearance eligibility as a real, independent gate, not a formality layered on top of a qualifying ASVAB score. For this rating specifically, the ICD 704 family-citizenship requirement is a distinct, additional gate beyond the general foreign-influence guideline -- a family member's citizenship in a non-low-risk country can be disqualifying independent of the applicant's own conduct.

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: VE + AR (Verbal Expression + Arithmetic Reasoning) composite >= 107. CONFIRMED this session via search (navycs.com and corroborating sources); this is a Navy per-rating subtest-sum composite, consistent with data/interest-taxonomy.json's note that the Navy uses per-rating subtest sums rather than a single named line score for this cluster.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: Eligibility for Top Secret clearance with continuous access to Sensitive Compartmented Information (SCI), determined by a Single Scope Background Investigation (SSBI) followed by reinvestigation every 5 years -- CONFIRMED this session via search. The applicant AND their immediate family must be U.S. citizens or from a country assessed low-risk under Intelligence Community Directive 704.

specialties.name: Machinist's Mate
specialties.what_it_is: MM absorbed most of the Boiler Technician (BT) rating on October 1, 1996 (steam-plant operations) and, following a 2013 Navy rating realignment, absorbed roughly 2,000 Engineman (EN) sailors whose primary skills were in auxiliary-engineering systems — auxiliary shipboard machinery work now falls under MM. The EN rating itself was NOT eliminated: it continues to exist as a smaller, narrower rating focused specifically on diesel propulsion for amphibious ships and smaller craft. Correction to this project's taxonomy: data/interest-taxonomy.json lists EN as a separate example specialty for this cluster; this session's research indicates EN still exists but in a substantially narrowed scope post-2013, with most of its former auxiliary-machinery scope now under MM — flagged as a taxonomy nuance rather than silently reconciled. A separate, full EN SpecialtyRecord was not built this session given the 10-web-call budget; MM is the representative record for Navy shipboard mechanical/engineering work.

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: UNVERIFIED — this session did not locate a specific MM ASVAB subtest-sum requirement (consistent with data/interest-taxonomy.json's note that the Navy uses per-rating subtest sums rather than a single published line score for most ratings in this cluster). navycs.com and mynavyhr.navy.mil pages exist but were not fetched to extract the exact composite this session. Confirm the current cutoff with a recruiter or the Navy COOL/MM rating page directly.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: Not typically required for surface-fleet MM; submarine-track assignments commonly involve additional screening given the closed operating environment, though this session did not independently confirm a formal clearance requirement.

specialties.name: Musician
specialties.what_it_is: Selection runs through the Navy Music Program (NMP) audition: performance of the National Anthem plus prepared repertoire across multiple styles, major/minor scales and arpeggios, sight-reading of standard sheet music, and (for vocalists) mic technique and stage presence. Per NMP materials, the large majority of successful candidates already hold a bachelor's degree or higher in music — the audition, not the ASVAB, is the real competitive barrier.

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: A figure of '31 on QT' was reported by one secondary source (navycs.com) but could not be independently confirmed against a primary Navy source this session, and sits awkwardly against this project's general finding that Navy ratings are qualified by per-rating ASVAB subtest sums rather than named line scores (interest-taxonomy.json) — treat as UNVERIFIED. What IS confirmed: both the ASVAB and a live audition are required, and the audition is the substantive gate.

specialties.code: MM(N) / EM(N) / ET(N) — Navy Nuclear Field (shipboard/submarine reactor-plant operation; entrants are assigned one of these three ratings, all trained through the same nuclear pipeline)
specialties.name: Nuclear Field (Machinist's Mate, Electrician's Mate, and Electronics Technician — Nuclear)
specialties.what_it_is: Entrants are assigned one of three ratings — Machinist's Mate (Nuclear, MM(N)), Electrician's Mate (Nuclear, EM(N)), or Electronics Technician (Nuclear, ET(N)) — based on program needs and candidate preference/aptitude, all of whom complete the same core nuclear-pipeline schools before diverging into rating-specific fleet duties. Nuclear Field also feeds Navy's submarine force specifically (a submarine-volunteer track requiring Basic Enlisted Submarine School) as well as nuclear-powered surface ships (aircraft carriers).

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: Navy has no single named line score system; the Nuclear Field (NF) program instead uses ASVAB-derived composites that are, per this project's binding research brief, among the HIGHEST composite requirements in the entire U.S. military. Two qualification tracks (per navycs.com/Navy Advanced Programs Test materials, searched 2026-07-12): Alpha (NFA) — qualifies directly with one relevant composite (reported as VE+AR+MK+MC) ≥ 252, with AR+MK ≥ 110 also required, and no further testing needed. Bravo (NFB) — qualifies with the relevant composites ≥ 225 (rather than 252), but must additionally sit and pass the Navy Advanced Programs Test (NAPT), a 2-hour supplementary exam, with a minimum score of 50. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level — these figures are corroborated across multiple secondary/test-prep sources but were not independently confirmed via a direct fetch of a primary Navy/MyNavyHR nuclear-program page this session; confirm current cutoffs with a recruiter or Navy COOL before relying on them.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: Nuclear Field candidates undergo a rigorous background investigation and a personal interview process historically associated with Naval Reactors (the program with roots in Admiral Rickover's nuclear-propulsion program); a Secret clearance or higher is commonly reported as a practical requirement given access to reactor-plant technical data. Not independently confirmed to primary-source level this session — flagged as trained-knowledge/secondary-corroborated rather than freshly sourced.

COOL lists 27 related credentials you would still have to earn separately, including Certified Safety Professional (CSP), CompTIA A+, CompTIA Network+.

specialties.name: Sonar Technician (Surface)
specialties.what_it_is: Operates and repairs surface-ship sonar systems and antisubmarine-warfare fire-control equipment for underwater surveillance and detection. Source lists two accession-program variants with different minimums: an Advanced Electronics Field (AEF) track and a five-year-obligation (5YO) track.

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: AR+MK+EI+GS>=222 (AEF track; source separately lists a 5YO track at AR+MK+EI+GS>=223 -OR- AR+MK+VE+AO>=228)

specialties.name: Special Warfare Boat Operator
specialties.what_it_is: Crews high-speed combatant craft (SWCC) to insert and extract special-operations forces and conduct maritime interdiction and coastal patrol.

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: AR+VE>=105 and MC>=51 -OR- AR+2MK+GS>=210 and MC>=51

specialties.name: Special Warfare Operator
specialties.what_it_is: Navy SEAL rating conducting unconventional warfare, direct-action raids, reconnaissance, and amphibious-beachhead operations across sea, air, and land.

specialties.name: Torpedoman's Mate
specialties.what_it_is: Responsible for loading, unloading, and storing torpedoes, Tomahawk missiles, and countermeasure devices on submarines, and maintains high-pressure air and hydraulic launching systems.

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: Navy ratings do not publish a single named 'line score' the way the Army does — data/interest-taxonomy.json documents this as 'no line score — per-rating subtest sums.' One secondary source (cogn-iq.org, retrieved 2026-07-12) reports a subtest-sum requirement of VE+MK+CS ≥155 for YN. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level — mynavyhr.navy.mil returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch in this project's prior culinary-cluster research (data/specialties-culinary.json) and was not re-attempted this session given the ~10-web-call budget; confirm the current requirement with a recruiter or Navy COOL.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: Not typically required for general YN duty; UNVERIFIED whether assignments handling classified correspondence, military-justice/legal records, or flag-level staff work carry an elevated Secret-clearance requirement — not independently confirmed this session.
